##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol: it's everywhere... but going...Google Maps!

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2009-11-17T13:22:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TBDMm.12279$Sw5.6915@newsfe16.iad>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<7mfsttF3h3s88U4@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<df403767-b191-483d-8191-fd1b4fae44eb@j24g2000yqa.googlegroups.com> <7mfsttF3h3s88U4@mid.individual.net>`

```
Robert Doerfler wrote:
>>I would like to share this little example that I've created of how to
>>integrate Powercobol 5.x and Google Maps.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Emerson,

Robert has a good point - it is a pain in the arse to have to register 
for 'informative' sites.

I'm greatly irritated by sites that seem to feel it is necessary. Are 
you like the others protecting the Crown Jewels ? Not your site 
specifically, but this is the sort of nonsense you get :

(1) Enter a user name - response - "Can't use that, it's already in use".

(2) Enter first and last names and e-mail address - that's OK if it *IS* 
your intention to keep people up-to-date on changes to your site. If you 
don't intend to keep them abreast of your changes, (by a round robin 
e-mail list), then name and e-mail address are irrelevant.

(3) Enter Password - Question - 'Exactly WHY ?' - and in your case I 
have tried to sign up TWICE and then when I Login, am informed "Password 
is invalid", (AND I am aware of case sensitivity). Take it as read I 
wont be attempting to sign up for a THIRD time. Pity - 'cos I'm 
interested in things OO COBOL.

If you insist on Passwords - let 'em key in what the hell they like - 
then store it in your records as UPPERCASE for verification.

I think you have gone over the top in all the caveats about using the 
site. Howz about, 'This is a site for developers with samples of source 
code, as is, without any warranties. Please use as you see fit. When you 
use or pass the source on, kindly acknowledge where you obtained the 
source from....".


Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol: it's everywhere... but going...Google Maps!

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-11-18T13:52:34+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mguqkF3hpt3cU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<df403767-b191-483d-8191-fd1b4fae44eb@j24g2000yqa.googlegroups.com> <7mfsttF3h3s88U4@mid.individual.net> <TBDMm.12279$Sw5.6915@newsfe16.iad>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> Robert Doerfler wrote:
>>> I would like to share this little example that I've created of how
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> for 'informative' sites.

I agree, it very often is. (I try to make it as painless as possible on the 
sites where I need to get a registration from people, but there is always 
some degree of inconvenience.)
>
> I'm greatly irritated by sites that seem to feel it is necessary.

The problem is that if you are giving stuff away, you like to have a list of 
people who downloaded it so you can update them or offer them something else 
along the same lines in the future. (But the last thing you want to do is 
bug them every five minutes with some "new"  product. I hate it when a site 
I'm registered on sends me email about an "important" new product that is 
not in the least bit important to me... :-))

 The problem often arises when web developers use "encapsulated" or 
"pre-fabricated" registration components, without REALLY looking at what the 
code does or requires, and are then at the mercy of what that code requires. 
For this reason I write all site registrations from scratch, and have 
complete control over how it works. If I find it unwieldy, I simply change 
it. After having done a number of these over the years, I now have it down 
to a fine art. To be fair, database manipulation on the web is not simple 
for people who are used to a desktop environment, and, of course, any kind 
of registration usually requires DB access. (I guess you COULD write a flat 
file, but I can't think why you would do that...)

There is a balance between giving people open access to your site and not 
knowing who did what, and getting them to register so you have at least an 
idea of what they're after, and what they downloaded.

Many people share your frustration with Registration. The COBOL 21 site has 
taken over 3000 unique hits, but only just over 100 registrations.

(I have never circulated the registration list with anything, but there are 
some new freebies coming up, and these people will be the first to know... 
:-))


Are
> you like the others protecting the Crown Jewels ? Not your site
> specifically, but this is the sort of nonsense you get :
>
> (1) Enter a user name - response - "Can't use that, it's already in
> use".
Using email is usually a good unique identifier, but this gets tedious to 
enter. I usually offer a field where you can make up your own "shortcut" 
identifier (people often use their initials or a nickname)  for commercial 
sites, and logon with that.

(See  http://primacomputing.co.nz/BravadoAdmin/ for an example of this, but 
please don't visit this site unnecessarily. Please DO click the link on to 
the Bravado public site. (I did the glowing logo for this... :-))

> (2) Enter first and last names and e-mail address - that's OK if it
> *IS* your intention to keep people up-to-date on changes to your
> site. If you don't intend to keep them abreast of your changes, (by a
> round robin e-mail list), then name and e-mail address are irrelevant.
>
There's a fine balance here. Most people don't want to be inundated with 
promotional emails, just because they registered. I'm registered with a 
certain site in Europe where I purchased some of their products on ONE 
occasion 4 months ago. Now I get email from them EVERY other day, telling me 
about free offers (that are NOT actually free because they charge a fortune 
for "mailing"...). On the other hand, as you say, if people on the list are 
NEVER mailed then there is little point in making them register.


> (3) Enter Password - Question - 'Exactly WHY ?' - and in your case I
> have tried to sign up TWICE and then when I Login, am informed
> "Password is invalid", (AND I am aware of case sensitivity).

I agree entirely. It is really annoying when a site acts like "Nanny" and 
decides they know what your password should comply with, and then write this 
perception into software... It is MY password, if it isn't secure, that's MY 
problem. (I take some care with financial sites, but other than that, if 
someone gets my password, so what?) It isn't just case sensitivity, either; 
some sites insist on a minimum length and a particular mix of characters... 
I don't see the point. If you are going to make it hard for me to register, 
guess what?

>Take it
> as read I wont be attempting to sign up for a THIRD time. Pity - 'cos
> I'm interested in things OO COBOL.

Completely understandable.
>
> If you insist on Passwords - let 'em key in what the hell they like -

Absolutely.

> then store it in your records as UPPERCASE for verification.

Actually, I encrypt it before it is stored and case sensitivity is easily 
dealt with after unencryption on retrieval.
>
> I think you have gone over the top in all the caveats about using the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> obtained the source from....".

I think that kind of decision needs to be made BEFORE even building a site. 
It is good to have a clear idea of what your reasons are for building a Web 
site, before you start. ANY Web site involves a fair deal of work, even 
though the modern tools are getting better and better.

Many people seriously underestimate the work involved (not saying Emerson 
did that...)

If you want people to download stuff, you don't really need a site at all. 
All you need is a page with links, but that soon becomes extended to telling 
people something about the things on offer, and, before you know it, you 
have a Web site... :-)

In this specific instance, I went and had a look at Emerson's site.

I' m deeply sympathetic to ANYONE who is building a Web site so I try to 
suspend judgement and just let it flow until I have a general feeling about 
it.

Here are my personal opinions about this site (none of this is intended to 
wound; hopefully, it may stimulate some needed review...):

1. I think all of Jimmy's criticisms are fair and accurate.

2. I like the colour scheme and the general clear and simple layout.

3. I DON'T like the pidgin English on the home page and it looks to me like 
this template was made in China or somewhere. However, that is just a 
personal thing which happens because I write a lot; many people really don't 
care about how things are worded. For me, I simply leave ANY site where the 
English is substandard. Publish in your native tongue, or publish in English 
and get it edited by someone who is an English speaker.

4. I can't see ANY reason to make people register so they can provide you 
with information that YOU want, like what kind of COBOL they are using. I'd 
let them select the option. Perhaps you might only look at what they 
selected when they register (to prevent people just frivolously clicking 
radio buttons and skewing the result), but it is "unfriendly" to tell them 
they can't vote unless they register, IF you put the poll right on the home 
page.

5. I liked the statement that it is a work in progress and this deflected a 
number of criticisms I would otherwise have made.

I skimmed through all the verbiage and legalese and I agree with Jimmy. This 
is SO restrictive that I wouldn't even consider submitting code to it. More 
importantly, this just lends an unfriendly and sterile atmosphere to the 
whole site, which is unfortunate.

(It is pretty obvious that this is just boilerplate where they have replaced 
a placeholder with "100 Cool things...."  The repetition was intensely 
irritating. Using boilerplate like this sends a wrong message to your 
visitors. If you "can't be bothered" to write something simple and 
meaningful in this area, then why should anybody be bothered to search your 
site and browse it? I'd replace this gobbledygook with a simple clear 
English statement.)

The site is REALLY about a platform to show COBOL doing cool things. But 
where are these cool things?  It says there are only a few so far, (fair 
enough) but why isn't the "coolest" one in my face on the home page? The 
idea is to show what the cool thing is (a demo is a good idea and an 
interactive demo is a brilliant idea... see 
http://primacomputing.co.nz/cobol21/S2NTestServer.aspx ), then, when they've 
played with it a bit, if they are interested you can talk about downloads 
and registration...

I'd really like to see much more being offered up front; stuff that will 
engage people, and encourage them to dig deeper.

So far, it is going "nowhere"... :-)

Pete.




>
>
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **References:** `<df403767-b191-483d-8191-fd1b4fae44eb@j24g2000yqa.googlegroups.com> <7mfsttF3h3s88U4@mid.individual.net> <TBDMm.12279$Sw5.6915@newsfe16.iad> <7mguqkF3hpt3cU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Robert/James/Pete,

Thank you for the feedback. I'll implement some suggestions, but this
does not include the removal of registration. If you are having any
problems with the registration process send me an email ( emersonlopes
at gmail com ) I'll try to fix the issue asap.

I apologize for the "engrish" usage in the site...english is not my
first language. If you see something wrong (grammar, for instance)
please let me know and I'll try to fix as soon as possible.

I agree that the "terms of use" text is too wordy. I'll try to put
this in plain english soon.

I've used a template to create the site, so I know that it has poor
usability, but the goal now is just to have a place where you can
download content and read basic directions. I hope to improve content
(and look and feel) in the next months.

There will be dynamic examples (including web services and OO Cobol +
Flex), but this is not a top priority for me at this moment. I'm not
selling any product or service, and I'm not making any money with this
work. This a private, non-funded project, so things will improve very
slowly.

I'm hardly protecting anything. The source code is available at
codeplex.com website (which does not require any registration). There
are things that I'm planning to do that will require user unique
identification (e.g. OO Cobol driving site personalisation). Better to
have this from the very first moment than impose such thing later.

Finally, the front page will change to a blog format, keeping a
history of things published in the site. This is something that will
take place this weekend, but the text "work in progress" will be there
for a long time...

Regards, Emerson
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol: it's everywhere... but going...Google Maps!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-11-20T13:05:57+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mm4r6F3ii2eaU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<df403767-b191-483d-8191-fd1b4fae44eb@j24g2000yqa.googlegroups.com> <7mfsttF3h3s88U4@mid.individual.net> <TBDMm.12279$Sw5.6915@newsfe16.iad> <7mguqkF3hpt3cU1@mid.individual.net> <de502b39-c89c-4304-ab4e-9ad837cbb42f@e23g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <6D1Nm.36163$de6.18063@newsfe21.iad> <cb6c5644-63d4-4499-a6b1-767f75951d12@33g2000vbe.googlegroups.com>`

```
Emerson wrote:
> Hello,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> modern tool to develop state-of-art applications.

Unfortunately, that boat has sailed.

The people who should have boarded it 20 years ago didn't, and so their 
influence was lost on the OO world.

These days there are much better tools than COBOL for developing OO 
solutions.

Nevertheless, it is a worthwhile aspiration and I wish you every success 
with it.

I used OO COBOL for around 15 years and found it very useful, especially for 
building COM components and I am still running some of these in the .NET 
environment through InterOP services.

I really enjoyed working with COBOL and have a "soft spot" for it, but the 
harsh realities of modern technology cannot be denied when it comes to 
making a living or providing software for money. The world has voted with 
its feet and embraced the OO paradigm. That's bad news for COBOL.

You CAN use COBOL in OO environments, but it doesn't make commercial sense 
to do so.

The main problems with it are:

1. It is labour intensive to maintain (thousands of lines, as opposed to 
hundreds or even dozens... - using a component based approach can ameliorate 
some of this, but, in general, it costs more to maintain COBOL than it does 
C#, VB, or Java.  - I can do things in C# with a single statement that take 
a paragraph, or even a page, in COBOL).

2. It is expensive to buy (other OO tools and languages are free, and more 
powerful than COBOL in many ways.) They are designed around the OO paradigm; 
COBOL had it bolted on. (That is not to say that the "bolting" was not an 
outstanding piece of software engineering; it was. But it's still Jake the 
Peg with his extra leg...)

3. Support for it is generally poor and very expensive for what you get. In 
contrast, free support for C# is immediate via the Net and usually 
comprehensive, with many examples of code and conceptual backgrounds that 
simply aren't available for COBOL.

4. It is hard to get people skilled in OO COBOL (this represents a serious 
management risk.None of the managers I talk to would consider using COBOL as 
an OO tool for development, although some are concerned about their 
procedural COBOL legacy, and some are happy to keep COBOL for batch 
processing. The feeling is that existing programmers can pick up batch COBOL 
pretty easily, but OO COBOL is too convoluted to be worth the effort.)

That leaves the hobby and enthusiast sector.

Which brings us back to this forum... :-)

Of the people who come here, there are less than a handful using OO COBOL at 
all, and of that small "palmful" maybe one or two who are actually making a 
living from it in a commercial environment, as their main development 
language.

I would urge anyone in this category to post here and make me wrong. I 
really wish it was a more significant group, but experience over decades of 
posting here has shown it not to be.

One thing that isn't in doubt is the passion for COBOL that many people 
feel, and for that reason alone your undertaking to post 100 cool things 
COBOL can do, is a good one.

Life isn't always about commerce. :-)

On that happy note, let me mention in closing that I dined last night with a 
friend at a recently opened Brazilian Churrasco here in Tauranga. The 
weather here is moving into Summer and the evenings are warm and pleasant. 
We had superb food served by friendly gauchos in traditional garb and they 
explained the various Brazilian delicacies. The meat (this is not a 
restaurant for vegetarians, so I was in my element...:-)) was cooked on 
skewers over a charcoal grill and carved at the table. They brought 11 
different offerings ranging through lamb, chicken, pork, prawns, pineapple, 
and the specialty beef, all perfectly cooked and served at the table so it 
is always fresh and hot. They kind of kept an eye on the diners and, as they 
noticed people had finished one offering, they presented another one.

I love good food and this was a delight, but the highlight for me was the 
caipiranho. :-) This is fresh lime and sugar ground up to release the oils 
and then covered with ice and Brazilian Schnapps poured over the lot. 
Sipping this on a warm evening just makes the world a better place... :-)  I 
had 4 of these and suffered no ill effects; this morning I feel fine. I'll 
definitely be making some of these for guests over the holiday period and 
will try and locate some Brazilian Schnapps later today... (I'm sure the 
people at the restaurant will supply it if I can't find it in the liquor 
store.)

>
> I'm glad that you found my stuff relevant :) I'm planning to publish
> more nice things (the list is pretty extensive, but time is short,
> specially when I do not make money out of Cobol development).

Labours of love are satisfying when completed, but very demanding while 
under development. Good luck with your project.

>
> Yes, I'm a brazilian :) where else in the world you would find Cobol
> lovers ?...don't answer ;)

COBOL is alive and well in Brazil, South America generally, and Germany.

(I get private mail on COBOL related matters mostly from these countries and 
occasionally from India or Pakistan.)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol: it's everywhere... but going...Google Maps!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Emerson <emersonlopes@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-11-20T06:21:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e6c7f23f-8cb2-47d6-82ab-0a96ffbf4c67@m20g2000vbp.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<df403767-b191-483d-8191-fd1b4fae44eb@j24g2000yqa.googlegroups.com> <7mfsttF3h3s88U4@mid.individual.net> <TBDMm.12279$Sw5.6915@newsfe16.iad> <7mguqkF3hpt3cU1@mid.individual.net> <de502b39-c89c-4304-ab4e-9ad837cbb42f@e23g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <6D1Nm.36163$de6.18063@newsfe21.iad> <cb6c5644-63d4-4499-a6b1-767f75951d12@33g2000vbe.googlegroups.com> <7mm4r6F3ii2eaU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Hi Pete,

Don't you abuse on Caipirinhas :) The hangover is frequently worst
than anything that you have had in the past :) ...I KNOW BETTER!

I think that the whole point is that Cobol is not going to disappear
any soon. Take a look in big companies (I work for one of them, very
big indeed). They are all creating "legacy modernization" tools that
not necessarily get rid of Cobol code, but actually replace stuff like
CICS code with OO objects that do the job or offering rehosting for
non-CICS apps or even replacing UI with modern solutions while keep
legacy wrapped (but still there) with Java/C# code doing the wrapper.

One of the driving forces behind this movement is SOA that is allowing
seemlessly integration between heterogenous envs, another one is the
lack of reason to keep old costly mainframes when cheaper multi-core
non-stop servers can do the job.

Does is it represents a era for Cobol? I would not go that far, but
definitely it is pushing IT budgets into Cobol services when all that
you used to see in the past was "keep this until we rewrite in...",
NOT!.

We have a very active forum in Brazil where you can find people
dealing with OO Cobol+XML+GUI+Web+COM+.Net+etc (there is also people
working with MSCobol 2.x...alas) in a daily basis (http://
br.groups.yahoo.com/group/cobol/messages). The forum is in
portuguese...nothing is perfect :)

Regards, Emerson

On Nov 19, 7:05 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

…[118 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol: it's everywhere... but going...Google Maps!

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-11-21T16:04:22+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mp3lpF3ir31fU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<df403767-b191-483d-8191-fd1b4fae44eb@j24g2000yqa.googlegroups.com> <7mfsttF3h3s88U4@mid.individual.net> <TBDMm.12279$Sw5.6915@newsfe16.iad> <7mguqkF3hpt3cU1@mid.individual.net> <de502b39-c89c-4304-ab4e-9ad837cbb42f@e23g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <6D1Nm.36163$de6.18063@newsfe21.iad> <cb6c5644-63d4-4499-a6b1-767f75951d12@33g2000vbe.googlegroups.com> <7mm4r6F3ii2eaU1@mid.individual.net> <e6c7f23f-8cb2-47d6-82ab-0a96ffbf4c67@m20g2000vbp.googlegroups.com>`

```
Emerson wrote:
> Hi Pete,
>
> Don't you abuse on Caipirinhas :) The hangover is frequently worst
> than anything that you have had in the past :) ...I KNOW BETTER!

Hi Emerson,

I definitely wasn't abusing them; they are fantastic!

Thank you for tactfully correcting my spelling; it is a new word for me but 
I have a feeling it will become a loved one...

As for hangovers, I think it must be something in the wiring, but I have 
NEVER had a headache, let alone a hangover, ever, in my life. There is an 
old adage that if you "mix the grain and grape" you will have a terrible 
time the next day. Not me. I drink whatever I feel like drinking and in the 
course of an evening that could be beer, wine, and spirits (though I have 
limited tase in spirits, a fine single malt is (and has been) wasted on 
me...

Jack Daniels and Cardenal Mendoza brandy are my favourites (I have to import 
the Cardenal Mendoza myself because no-one in New Zealand carries it... it 
works out at an outrageous price per bottle but I keep it as a treat and it 
is one of the very few self-indulgences I have). A few years back I made the 
pilgrimage I had been promising myself for nigh on 40 years, to Lynchburg, 
Tennessee (a beautiful little town) for a day in the Jack Daniels 
distillery. It was simply incredible and I heartily recommend it to anyone 
holidaying in the USA. I hope sometime to get to Oporto and meet the family 
that produce Cardenal Mendoza... :-)

I now have a bottle of  Cacacha and caipirinhos are on the menu... :-)


>
> I think that the whole point is that Cobol is not going to disappear
> any soon.

I agree. It was never going to disappear overnight. I predicted in 1996 that 
by 2015 COBOL would not be used as a main development language and the 
"COBOL shops" still prevalent at the time, would disappear.

That hasn't happened yet, but I see no reason to revise or withdraw this 
date. In fact, I hadn't reckoned with Kurzweil's accelerated rate of change 
and the process I imagined then, is much further ahead now than I thought it 
would be.



>Take a look in big companies (I work for one of them, very
> big indeed). They are all creating "legacy modernization" tools that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> legacy wrapped (but still there) with Java/C# code doing the wrapper.

I was an original advocate of this approach and am currently selling tools I 
wrote to do that... :-)

There is no point in throwing away perfectly good COBOL code when it can be 
rewrapped and made into objects that can work extremely well with other 
languages on other platforms.

Please have a look at http://primacomputing.co.nz/cobol21  and, in 
particular, http://primacomputing.co.nz/cobol21/mig.aspx which outlines the 
strategy and click the PRIMA Toolset link from that page to 
http://primacomputing.co.nz/cobol21/PRIMAToolset.aspx

I think these 3 pages cover COBOL modernisation (and, in particular my own 
views on it) pretty well.

It isn't about getting rid of COBOL (although, long term, many companies 
will want to do that), it is about NOT writing off an existing investment 
when you don't actually have to.   We've had discussions here in this forum 
about this. Some sites will decide to simply write off what they have and 
start from scratch, using a package or component based approach, others will 
have critical Legacy code that they really would like to retain.

Sometimes there just aren't the resources to immediately rewrite the core 
business processing; wrapping existing COBOL buys time.

It is important to remember also that it isn't only big companies that have 
COBOL software. I am catering (at the moment) to SMEs (Small/Medium 
Enterprises) who simply don't have the resources to take some of the options 
the larger corporates can.

My original goal was to simply write tools to assist my own conversion away 
from COBOL to C# and .NET. When I did this, some people heard I had done it 
and asked about it so I decided to do more work on the Toolset with a view 
to making it available to small businesses for a price that would be less 
than the .NET COBOL compiler... :-)

I've been promising myself to retire from the travelling and stress that IT 
consultancy entails for over 10 years now and focusing on a single product 
set enables me to do that. I'll never get rich from PRIMA but that isn't why 
I started it. It enables me to work from comfortable surroundings (I can go 
to a deserted beach and write code if I want to...) doing something I love, 
and actually watching people win. I get occasional help with some of the 
"grunt" work from some local IT students and that is very satisfying as 
well. The Clients are enthusiastic and I have emails that are almost 
embarrasing in their glowing praise of the Toolset and the support they get 
from PRIMA. I can see me doing this for as long as I can see to type and 
avoid going doolally with old age... :-)

There is still some travelling but it is a combination of business and 
pleasure so I really don't mind it. I'll be in the USA in February to see 
the implementation of a new system running on the Web and .NET using a SQL 
Server DB that was created and loaded entirely by my tools from the old 
indexed files, and being accessed through a Data Access Layer of Objects, 
also created by the Toolset. This was an old COBOL system  (prior to COBOL 
'85) and I extended the Toolset to handle some of the COBOL :-) It's funny 
to think of COBOL '74 invoking objects to run on .NET in 2009. I wonder what 
the original programmers would make of it all...

>
> One of the driving forces behind this movement is SOA that is allowing
> seemlessly integration between heterogenous envs, another one is the
> lack of reason to keep old costly mainframes when cheaper multi-core
> non-stop servers can do the job.

I am a big fan of SOA even though it has fallen out of "fashion". From the 
moment I wrote my first web service and watched VS 2008 generate all the 
WSDL and SOAP, I was sold :-). Previously, I had done some work with web 
based payments to a secure server in a Bank that required hand coded SOAP 
and I struggled with it for a couple of weeks before it finally worked (I 
was teaching myself the concepts and details of SOAP as well as getting my 
head round the application).  I won't be putting my hand up for any more 
SOAP if I can help it... then along comes VS 2008 and it simply generates it 
for you in seconds! Great stuff!

I like the general concept of "services" that are platform independent.
>
> Does is it represents a era for Cobol? I would not go that far, but
> definitely it is pushing IT budgets into Cobol services when all that
> you used to see in the past was "keep this until we rewrite in...",
> NOT!.

Certainly there is truth in that.  But as these COBOL services need 
modification I think you will see a move to rewrite them in something else. 
It is like I said before: You CAN do it in COBOL but there are other, better 
ways. Certainly wrapping COBOL as objects can buy you time, and that is very 
important.

>
> We have a very active forum in Brazil where you can find people
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> portuguese...nothing is perfect :)

There's nothing wrong with Portuguese (or any other language... )

It's just difficult for English speakers to understand without suitable 
training. :-)

I love the English language, but I certainly don't see it as "superior" or 
"better" than any other language. (I currently speak 4 languages moderately 
well, and can "read" another three, although Portuguese is not one of them). 
I didn't acquire these skills because I am a scholar; it was because I was 
living in communities where they were spoken and I wanted to get the richest 
possible experience of those cultures during my time there.(Usually at least 
a couple of years...)

In all cases, I never had formal lessons, just taught myself and got help 
from friends I made along the way.

I've had a colourful life, I guess... looking forward to some peace and 
quiet now...:-)

(One of the nice things about living in Tauranga is that you can walk down 
the road without the risk of being shot, or go to the movies and know that 
no deluded fanatic is going to incinerate the cinema; that has not always 
been the case in my life... I don't know how long it will stay that way as 
the world seems to be descending into chaos, but my plan is to enjoy it 
while I can :-))

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol: it's everywhere... but going...Google Maps!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cncdg5dnf2ghb8co3u213mpr40pn1ns36f@4ax.com>`

```
On Fri, 20 Nov 2009 13:05:57 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:


>Unfortunately, that boat has sailed.

Unfortunately for me maybe - I'm not sure it is unfortunate for the
industry.

>The people who should have boarded it 20 years ago didn't, and so their 
>influence was lost on the OO world.

I really don't se how it could have happened.    The big users of
CoBOL wanted stability.    Those wanting new ways of doing things
wanted cheap tools and less control (that tends to be the way
revolutions happen - control comes later).

...

>The main problems with it are:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>a paragraph, or even a page, in COBOL).

I'm not a big proponent of line efficiency myself.

>2. It is expensive to buy (other OO tools and languages are free, and more 
>powerful than COBOL in many ways.) They are designed around the OO paradigm; 
>COBOL had it bolted on. (That is not to say that the "bolting" was not an 
>outstanding piece of software engineering; it was. But it's still Jake the 
>Peg with his extra leg...)

This and #4 in your list are intimately related, and are IMHO, the
reason OO CoBOL isn't even an afterthought.    Lack of modern tools
and lack of support are results of these two.

>3. Support for it is generally poor and very expensive for what you get. In 
>contrast, free support for C# is immediate via the Net and usually 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>pretty easily, but OO COBOL is too convoluted to be worth the effort.)

Java was free, and college kids could do whatever they wanted in their
spare time with Java.    People could use HTML to start some cheap
business applications then apply Java to make it useful without going
though IS budgets and control.     Sure that had its costs, but they
were local costs.

>That leaves the hobby and enthusiast sector.

Hobbyists and enthusiasts in programming languages start off with
cheap and free.

>Which brings us back to this forum... :-)
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>language.

I wouldn't be surprised if the number doing so in a mainframe
environment is zero.

>I would urge anyone in this category to post here and make me wrong. I 
>really wish it was a more significant group, but experience over decades of 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>noticed people had finished one offering, they presented another one.

I went to one of these restaurants Saturday, taking my son's family
out for their anniversary.


>> I'm glad that you found my stuff relevant :) I'm planning to publish
>> more nice things (the list is pretty extensive, but time is short,
>> specially when I do not make money out of Cobol development).

I have to say that I find it more interesting and fun than relevant
for me.    I will be doing very little CoBOL in our PeopleSoft
environment, and I don't do any business programming on PCs.    I can
retire now, but the longer I wait, the better my pension.   The
mainframe has another year before it goes away.
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 20 Nov 2009 21:57:33 GMT, Robert Doerfler <rocrash@gmx.de> wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> it.

I'm not sure that's entirely fair, Howard. I made a specific point of 
including Open COBOL into the cobol21 site, Bill produced an extremely 
useful document aimed at people who are not Linux literate, showing how to 
download it, and there are a number of sites that are linked to Open COBOL. 
If people haven't heard of it, it is because they haven't searched...

http://primacomputing.co.nz/cobol21/opencobol.aspx

Yes, you would need to be a dedicated COBOLler to get it, and it is 
primarily of interest to hobbyists at the moment, but at least one 
commercial installation (in Texas) is running it in a production 
environment.

Personally, I would be interested to use it as an interim migration tool if 
it had OO, but it doesn't, and it looks like this is not on the agenda. (I 
can understand this... there are a dedicated few people developing it and it 
is a labour of love...).

Pete.
```

```
On Tue, 24 Nov 2009 15:04:06 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>> Who starts off with OpenCOBOL?    First, they would have to have
>> *heard* of it, then they would have to have an application it was
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I'm not sure that's entirely fair, Howard. 

Fair in what way?    I'm speculating on those kids who are want to
take up programming on the cheap.    I expect most of these don't know
anything about CoBOL except that it is the way programming used to
happen on big mainframes.   (Which is true - but very incomplete)

>I made a specific point of 
>including Open COBOL into the cobol21 site, Bill produced an extremely 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>environment.

Dedicated COBOLers have already started off with CoBOL.

>Personally, I would be interested to use it as an interim migration tool if 
>it had OO, but it doesn't, and it looks like this is not on the agenda. (I 
>can understand this... there are a dedicated few people developing it and it 
>is a labour of love...).

It's neat - but the people who will look for it and find it are
already using CoBOL.     I don't see any reason for some kid starting
to learn to program to search for anything CoBOL.     

One of the big selling points of OO CoBOL is that it allows someone to
take existing code and fit it into an environment with different
languages.    That's not a selling point for someone who's wanting to
learn to program though.

I remember learning CoBOL over 40 years ago - starting off with the
Environment and Data divisions irritated that we were not getting to
the "programming" part.    I suspect the kids of today think the same
way - and they will find the tools that skip I/O (When do people
learning Java start accessing files?)

I doubt if people ever selected CoBOL as the language they wanted to
futz with (unless it was the language they already were most
comfortable with).    It has always made the most sense when we
actually have business data to work with.     
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol: it's everywhere... but going...Google Maps!

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-11-25T08:29:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rvhqg518okc5lmk61aliv7sldamskk8hmh@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<de502b39-c89c-4304-ab4e-9ad837cbb42f@e23g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <6D1Nm.36163$de6.18063@newsfe21.iad> <cb6c5644-63d4-4499-a6b1-767f75951d12@33g2000vbe.googlegroups.com> <7mm4r6F3ii2eaU1@mid.individual.net> <cncdg5dnf2ghb8co3u213mpr40pn1ns36f@4ax.com> <7mohmdF3iamtgU1@mid.individual.net> <vaalg5h5nia1qn8jf7aqug03m8i50v7721@4ax.com> <7n0t8fF3irdm2U1@mid.individual.net> <blvng55sklc09mlla2aclg1mm5b9l694bu@4ax.com> <7n4khuF3k8nvfU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Thu, 26 Nov 2009 00:59:56 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>>>> Who starts off with OpenCOBOL?    First, they would have to have
>>>> *heard* of it, then they would have to have an application it was
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>implied that no-one has heard of it.

I only meant to imply that people just starting to explore programming
have never heard of it.    Sorry for my lack of clarity.

...


>> I doubt if people ever selected CoBOL as the language they wanted to
>> futz with (unless it was the language they already were most
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>months I was a believer... :-)

I wonder how common that experience is.     (How about here on this
newsgroup?)

Sometimes a parent or spouse can be sort of sorry we forced our family
member to try something, as they finally become converted.   

Some old timers chose CoBOL because "that's where the jobs are", and
then discovered that it really works well (at least for the type of
work we were doing).     Some old timers chose CoBOL because that's
what their bosses told them to do.    

Few hobbyists will drag themselves kicking and screaming into CoBOL.
(Maybe a few who want to try *all* languages).     So few of them will
be come believers the way we did.

I saved up for a while for IBM's OO CoBOL on OS/2, but finally decided
there were other tools that were more valuable for my money. Obviously
this was before OpenCOBOL.     

I also have wanted to play around with various tools on my home Mac. I
sat down and tried to decide what I wanted to script with its
scripting tool, and didn't come up with anything.    What kinds of
toys would kids want to program?    Maybe a book catalog.   Certainly
applications for iPhones and its competitors.   Web pages.    Get good
enough to write games.

Business applications would not be high on their list.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol: it's everywhere... but going...Google Maps!

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-11-21T16:26:38+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mp4vgF3i4t70U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<df403767-b191-483d-8191-fd1b4fae44eb@j24g2000yqa.googlegroups.com> <7mfsttF3h3s88U4@mid.individual.net> <TBDMm.12279$Sw5.6915@newsfe16.iad> <7mguqkF3hpt3cU1@mid.individual.net> <de502b39-c89c-4304-ab4e-9ad837cbb42f@e23g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <6D1Nm.36163$de6.18063@newsfe21.iad> <cb6c5644-63d4-4499-a6b1-767f75951d12@33g2000vbe.googlegroups.com> <7mm4r6F3ii2eaU1@mid.individual.net> <cncdg5dnf2ghb8co3u213mpr40pn1ns36f@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

Interesting (as usual) response, Howard, and some good points.

Quick comments below...

> On Fri, 20 Nov 2009 13:05:57 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> industry.

That is an excellent point. I've never really considered what effect the 
"loss of COBOL" really has on commerce in general. I can see passionate 
arguments being made on both sides of that... :-)
>
>> The people who should have boarded it 20 years ago didn't, and so
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> revolutions happen - control comes later).

I'm not so sure. I reckon if there had been the same support on mainframe 
sites for OO that there had previously been for COBOL 2 (for example - slow 
at first but gathering momentum) - it would have resulted in programmers who 
were better prepared for the 21st century and it would have meant that COBOL 
would have been influencing other evolving OO languages (maybe...) as well 
as evolving itself. Of course we will never know, and as time goes by it 
becomes less important...))
>
> ...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'm not a big proponent of line efficiency myself.

As it happens, neither am I, but I AM a big proponent of reducing 
maintenance costs. Hand cobbling thousands of lines of code is not cost 
efficient.
>
>> 2. It is expensive to buy (other OO tools and languages are free,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> and lack of support are results of these two.

Well #4 is largely a result of my point about OO CBOL not being embraced by 
the COBOL community early on in the piece...
>
>> 3. Support for it is generally poor and very expensive for what you
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> were local costs.

Yes, I think that is pretty fair. Languages like Java and HTML (if you call 
that a "language") were POPULAR and easily accessible. COBOL was/is 
expensive and elitist.

It is interesting to me that some 40 million people have downloaded C# from 
MS. I can't see anyone doing that if they have no intention of learning to 
use it. I guess there is a perception amongst the young that "computers are 
cool" and "computer programming is REALLY cool". Probably, a large number 
will be writing games (MS has a site dedicated to helping kids who want to 
do this in C#)
, but in a population that large, there will be some who are exceptionally 
bright and will be looking for more cerebral satisfaction.

These will be the software engineers of tomorrow and we will look on their 
works and marvel... :-)

>
>> That leaves the hobby and enthusiast sector.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I believe there is at least one mainframe site (in Germany) where they 
are(or were, a few years back)  using OO COBOL, but in general, I think 
you're right.

>> I would urge anyone in this category to post here and make me wrong.
>> I really wish it was a more significant group, but experience over
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> out for their anniversary.

It's a blast, isn't it? :-)
>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> mainframe has another year before it goes away.

That last surprised me, Howard.

What will happen to all the IDMS stuff? Are they migrating it?  Network to 
Relational could be an interesting exercise... :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol: it's everywhere... but going...Google Maps!

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-11-21T18:06:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<he9a78$iql$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<df403767-b191-483d-8191-fd1b4fae44eb@j24g2000yqa.googlegroups.com> <7mm4r6F3ii2eaU1@mid.individual.net> <cncdg5dnf2ghb8co3u213mpr40pn1ns36f@4ax.com> <7mp4vgF3i4t70U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <7mp4vgF3i4t70U1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>Howard Brazee wrote:

[snip]

>> The big users of
>> CoBOL wanted stability.    Those wanting new ways of doing things
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>as evolving itself.

Mr Dashwood, the processes that produce Production-quality COBOL were/are 
(in some places) so stable, so painstaking that code 'old enough to vote' 
still running prudictively is something I regularly encounter.  The result 
was a large up-front investment in specs, conferences, designs, redesigns, 
unit testing and parallel testing... and then something that ran for 
decades.  There isn't, as far as I know, many other OO languages which can 
be shown to do the same.

As for the how COBOL 2 was developed... I've posted here (can't find it at 
the moment) of difficulties I had when I tried to implement COBOL '85 
constructs at client sites in the late 1990s.  Being refused for a 
decade-and-change is 'slow at first', indeed).

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol: it's everywhere... but going...Google Maps!

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-11-22T14:21:00+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mrhvsF3i0o3cU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<df403767-b191-483d-8191-fd1b4fae44eb@j24g2000yqa.googlegroups.com> <7mm4r6F3ii2eaU1@mid.individual.net> <cncdg5dnf2ghb8co3u213mpr40pn1ns36f@4ax.com> <7mp4vgF3i4t70U1@mid.individual.net> <he9a78$iql$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <7mp4vgF3i4t70U1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> encounter.

I know. Don't talk to me, talk to Howard, he said it. I let it pass, 
although my experience matches yours in this regard... :-) (That calendar is 
getting marks all over it... :-))


>  The result was a large up-front investment in specs,
> conferences, designs, redesigns, unit testing and parallel testing...
> and then something that ran for decades.  There isn't, as far as I
> know, many other OO languages which can be shown to do the same.

Ah, perhaps your experience with OO languages is a little limited? Certainly 
there isn't much around in OO that has been running for DECADES (Gosling and 
the Sun Green Team only got together 14 years ago), but there is certainly 
stuff that has been running satisfactorily for ONE decade (and shows no 
signs of not continuing to do so. )

While you can argue it hasn't stood the same test of time that COBOL 
applications have (there is very little extant that has), it HAS provided 
flexibilty and networked functionality which is much more difficult with 
COBOL. You don't actually need to have "a large up-front investment in 
specs, conferences, designs, redesigns, unit testing and parallel testing" 
in order to get something useful and reliable; that is just ONE way to 
achieve it. I have become persuaded that Agile methods are far better than 
the traditional Waterfall (SDLC) for developing commercial computer systems, 
but it is the destination that matters, rather than the journey...

>
> As for the how COBOL 2 was developed... I've posted here (can't find
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yeah... I was there too :-)

Pete.
```

```
In article <7mrhvsF3i0o3cU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

think that you know or do you only think that you know and I know you 
think this?

(see 'I cannot know what you mean, only what you say' as in previous 
posts)

>Don't talk to me, talk to Howard, he said it. I let it pass, 
>although my experience matches yours in this regard... :-) (That calendar is 
>getting marks all over it... :-))

It may be a joyful time, then... no need for grouchy marks.

>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Ah, perhaps your experience with OO languages is a little limited?

I believe I've referred to most of my experiences as 'limited'... nothing 
here to alert the wire services about.

>Certainly 
>there isn't much around in OO that has been running for DECADES (Gosling and 
>the Sun Green Team only got together 14 years ago), but there is certainly 
>stuff that has been running satisfactorily for ONE decade (and shows no 
>signs of not continuing to do so. )

A whole decade... did you know, Mr Dashwood, that I've seen newborn human 
children who showed absolutely no signs of puberty or associated secondary 
sexual characteristics for a whole decade?  Amazing what the difference a 
year or three can make in a person, a discipline or a computer language.

>
>While you can argue it hasn't stood the same test of time that COBOL 
>applications have (there is very little extant that has), it HAS provided 
>flexibilty and networked functionality which is much more difficult with 
>COBOL.

A scalpel does not provide the functionality of a chef's knife does not 
provide the functionality of a double-bitted axe does not provide the 
functionality of those huge machines which carve roads through forests, Mr 
Dashwood.  Each job has its toolbox.

>You don't actually need to have "a large up-front investment in 
>specs, conferences, designs, redesigns, unit testing and parallel testing" 
>in order to get something useful and reliable; that is just ONE way to 
>achieve it.

One doesn't need years of practise tossing darts to score a bull's-eye, 
either, Mr Dashwood... but in many cases it certainly does appear to help.

[snip]

>> As for the how COBOL 2 was developed... I've posted here (can't find
>> it at the moment) of difficulties I had when I tried to implement
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Yeah... I was there too :-)

Wow... was that *you* with the bushy-brow eyeglasses with the mustachoied 
false nose attached?

DD
```

```
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>>> Don't talk to me, talk to Howard, he said it. I let it pass,
>>> although my experience matches yours in this regard... :-) (That
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Swordfish.

Hey, that's how you're supposed to leave a speakeasy, not enter one!

[snip]

>>> Certainly
>>> there isn't much around in OO that has been running for DECADES
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>"hairy" but whether that is the effect of puberty or not remains in doubt...

A more hirsute appearance is but one manifestation of that particular 
stage, Mr Dashwood... increases in growth and strength are also 
encountered and long-bone ends change, as well.  What enters as a child 
becomes, with a bit of luck, a mature adult... but that might bring things 
back around to 1Cor.XIII:11.

[snip]

>>> You don't actually need to have "a large up-front investment in
>>> specs, conferences, designs, redesigns, unit testing and parallel
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>"that's how we've always done it".

Hmmmm... this seems to be a call for experimentation, Mr Dashwood, and a 
request for such is, in the small bits of the Business World I have seen, 
often a dicey matter; it is the constant struggle between 'Let's spend 
time and money seeing what we can do better' and 'if it ain't broke, don't 
fix it.' I do not claim to have or know of a reconciliation between the 
two.

DD
```

```
On Sat, 21 Nov 2009 16:26:38 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>> I went to one of these restaurants Saturday, taking my son's family
>> out for their anniversary.
>
>It's a blast, isn't it? :-)

Yep.

>>>> I'm glad that you found my stuff relevant :) I'm planning to publish
>>>> more nice things (the list is pretty extensive, but time is short,
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Relational could be an interesting exercise... :-)

It's just data.    I've been through lots of migrations (with
different companies and a variety of mainframes and minis), and they
have all involved analyzing the data and figuring out how to fit it
into the new structure.

I haven't been in a migration where the vaunted ability of CoBOL to
move from one environment to another was used.    We have always
converted data, but started over with code.

With OO, it may have been that more code would have been usable.   On
the other hand, it wouldn't have been code designed for the new
structures.     There is an argument to be made in favor of starting
over.
```

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
> The problem is that if you are giving stuff away, you like to have a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> me... :-))

What if the person doesn't WANT to be notified?

I'd like to take a look at this bit of code offered above - I might even 
have a use for it. But I will not register. If he can't live without my 
name, I can live without his code.

Take for example SourceForge. Bags and bags of interesting stuff and not a 
registration wand in sight. Consider (most of) Microsoft's web sights. 
Dumptruck loads of free profiles, templates, updates, programs, reference 
material, and no registrations.

If I do feel it's necessary to register, for example, to respond to some 
doofus writing an editorial in the Chicken Holler Gazette, I'll use an email 
address something like abuse@aol.gove or postmaster@whitehouse.gov.

Fuck 'em.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RFI

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** SeaSideSam <Sam@Sea.Side>
- **Date:** 2009-11-18T15:05:42-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73cb8$4b0461ae$62162ddc$9944@ALLTEL.NET>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<4b044ce3$0$5362$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`
- **References:** `<8e1d8$4b0374a3$62162ddc$21380@ALLTEL.NET> <4b039d05$0$5363$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <a3ad6$4b043646$62162ddc$24083@ALLTEL.NET> <4b044ce3$0$5362$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`

```
Paul wrote:
> I do apologize - I had meant that to go to your private e-mail and to be 
> amusing, not as rude at all. It was terribly thoughtless of me to post 
> that publicly though.

sorry paul; i don't understand what you are apologizing for.  i found 
nothing 'rude'.  if it will allow you feel better i accept your apology.

> 
> The compiler will be going to a Gulf War Vet I know trying to put his 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


this is 'my' story but i do believe that there are elements to it that 
are 'universal' among humans.

i left viet nam for the last time in december, 1971, not realizing that 
the best years of my life were already behind me.  when i left the 
service almost a year later i left feeling that everything was okay, 
believing that i was... untouched by the experience.

here i am, these years later, alone in a round house made of concrete 
and steel located at the end of the road where no one comes to visit... 
which is fine with me.

there is a couple locally who's life centers around alcohol and sex; 
who, in 1999, produced a girl child which they immediately began to 
share with any and all that would take her.  in 2004 they dropped the 
girl off with one of her grandmothers who had a full plate and no spare 
time or energy to take care of a 5 year old.  she asked me for help.

for the next ten months i was the child's primary care giver, her 
protector, her playmate and plaything, her friend and mentor.  her 
grandmother and i were more than a little surprised at what i achieved. 
  for ten months my life was all about caring for another person, not 
about 'me'.  those 10 months were the happiest and most satisfying 
period of time that i have had since viet nam.

learning how to program will most likely improve your friend in some 
ways, especially if there are employment opportunities available to him. 
  but to 'put his life back together' he will most benefit from an 
experience that allows him to live outside of the 'me'.  not an easy 
task.  i do not envy him what lies ahead.  i applaud any and all who 
take an interest in assisting him.  you are living outside of the 'me' paul.

sss



i did not write this to minimize, criticize, embarrass, or do or cause 
harm and or pain to anyone.  it is my nature to share and assist when 
and where possible.  my standard disclaimers apply.  1) take my advice 
for i'm not using it.  2)  my advice is worth exactly what you pay for it.
```

```
On Nov 19, 4:24 am, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> Paul wrote:
> > On 2009-11-18 15:05:42 -0600, SeaSideSam <S...@Sea.Side> said:
…[72 more quoted lines elided]…
> "I used to write COBOL...now I can do anything."

I used to have a really good friend whose favorite saying was "if it
is worth doing, it is worth getting paid for!",
and one of his corollaries was "if it is worth repeating, file off the
serial numbers and make it your own."

What he meant by that of course, was just that if you are going to
repeat someone else's phrase or saying,
be sure you *mean* it enough to own up to it. He hated when people
weasel worded things so as not to be
held responsible for what they said. <grin>

His name was Harry, and I have no idea whey he liked me at all, as I
was (according to him) a snot nosed kid
with a hell of a lot to learn.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RFI

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-11-20T01:41:00+00:00

```
In article <d5a1ffa3-3900-4109-8982-a3fd73f782a6@r5g2000yqb.googlegroups.com>,
PR  <paul.raulerson@gmail.com> wrote:
>On Nov 19, 4:24?am, "Pete Dashwood"
><dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>> Paul wrote:

[snip]

>> > Very cool indeed, and I like the way you put that - living outside of
>> > the "me." ? I think I will file the "serial numbers" off that saying
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>serial numbers and make it your own."

Here I am in agreement with Mr Dashwood (quick... someone mark a 
calendar); my training was such that a citation of another's work claimed 
as one's own was frequently called 'plagiarism' and grounds for having 
one's future efforts - no matter how worthwhile, original or sincere - 
summarily dismissed.

An aphorism which supports this - sourced to the great, prolific and 
long-lived Anonymous - goes something like 'Borrowed clothes, borrowed 
money, borrowed brains all will out'.  ('will out' is an English use 
indicating 'will be revealed' or 'will become obvious')

>
>What he meant by that of course, was just that if you are going to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>held responsible for what they said. <grin>

I'm not quite sure how this is supposed to make sense... but perhaps my 
experience is different; I have learnt a great deal from statements which 
began with 'Let it be argued this way:...' What becomes important is not 
matters of belief or opinion but ones of structure... definitions, 
postulates, common notions and then propositions to be proven or disproven 
according to them.  Then again... some folks have said I ain't much fun, 
neither.

>
>His name was Harry, and I have no idea whey he liked me at all, as I
>was (according to him) a snot nosed kid
>with a hell of a lot to learn.

I believe it was Freud who postulated that people dislike most in others 
what they dislike in themselves... ain't much of a stretch to disregard 
the 'dis'.

(or... perhaps he didn't like you and was trying to get you nailed in the 
future for plagiarising... stranger things have happened but please don't 
ask me to name three)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RFI

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** PR <paul.raulerson@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-11-20T08:58:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4b0de5e6-f3d2-422d-8857-56ad37785fe8@a32g2000yqm.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<8e1d8$4b0374a3$62162ddc$21380@ALLTEL.NET> <4b04d9ff$0$5345$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <7mkknqF3hbf1rU1@mid.individual.net> <d5a1ffa3-3900-4109-8982-a3fd73f782a6@r5g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <he4s3c$qoi$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Nov 19, 7:41 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
> In article <d5a1ffa3-3900-4109-8982-a3fd73f78...@r5g2000yqb.googlegroups.com>,
>
…[69 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

LOL! Doc, you and Pete are agreeing. I do love being somewhat at the
cause of that, cause you are both
people I respect and like.

Harry and I were great friends, but his nature was to appear as a
curmudgeon at every opportunity. He was a very fine human being. We
actually liked each other a lot.

On the other hand, I must point out, there is a difference between
what I am talking about and plagiarism. A vast difference. For
example:

trying to get you nailed
stranger things have happened
ain't much of a stretch


All of these statements were originally made in written form, verse,
or in some other medium which was attributable. However, they have
passed into common language now and none of us feel a need to
attribute them every time we use them.  "A sea change" is one of my
favorite sayings, and everyone "knows" what it means, but few actually
recognize it as coming from _The Tempest_. Nor do I feel ethically or
morally obligated to provide that information each time I use it.

What Harry always meant was much more along the lines of "say what you
mean, don't just repeat what the crowd is chanting." This also tied
into the idea that a writer, programmer, or any creative person only
rarely does anything really *new*.  Mostly they put together existing
ideas in odd new, usually entertaining, ways. I'm not sure I totally
agree with that, but hey - a painting today isn't much different from
a painting in the 1600's - just different inks and paints. Or
electronic analogs of the same, al la Photoshop. ;)

Also, there is a huge difference between using a saying like that, and
claiming to have originated it. That is the difference between common
usage and plagiarism.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RFI

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-11-20T18:25:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<he6mv5$rgu$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<8e1d8$4b0374a3$62162ddc$21380@ALLTEL.NET> <d5a1ffa3-3900-4109-8982-a3fd73f782a6@r5g2000yqb.googlegroups.com> <he4s3c$qoi$1@reader1.panix.com> <4b0de5e6-f3d2-422d-8857-56ad37785fe8@a32g2000yqm.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <4b0de5e6-f3d2-422d-8857-56ad37785fe8@a32g2000yqm.googlegroups.com>,
PR  <paul.raulerson@gmail.com> wrote:
>On Nov 19, 7:41?pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article <d5a1ffa3-3900-4109-8982-a3fd73f78...@r5g2000yqb.googlegroups.com>,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >> > and start using it myself. ;)

[snip]

>LOL! Doc, you and Pete are agreeing. I do love being somewhat at the
>cause of that, cause you are both
>people I respect and like.

In some instances, then, it seems as though you'se jes' easily amused.

>On the other hand, I must point out, there is a difference between
>what I am talking about and plagiarism. A vast difference. For
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>attribute them every time we use them.

There's the rub (Shak.), old boy... in the instances you've cited they 
have 'passed into common use' while Harry/you spoke of knowingly and 
intentionally obliterating the provenance.

[snip]

>What Harry always meant was much more along the lines of "say what you
>mean, don't just repeat what the crowd is chanting."

Meaning, as Wittgenstein tells it, is the result of interpretation.  I'm 
not sure what Harry intended by his phrase but I am familiar with the 
intention usually associated with 'filing off serial numbers'... and my 
experience is that it is not to provide for common use, let alone uses 
considered 'honorable' or 'legitimate' (in the sense of 'in accord with 
law').

>This also tied
>into the idea that a writer, programmer, or any creative person only
>rarely does anything really *new*.  Mostly they put together existing
>ideas in odd new, usually entertaining, ways.

To continue with the 'filing off the serial numbers' metaphor... I'm 
accustomed to hearing that most frequently associated with firearms or 
vehicles which have been stolen and which the filer does not want to be 
readily traced.  There's a bit of difference between taking different 
parts and building your own, I'd say, and taking a completed object and 
altering it to remove attribution.

>I'm not sure I totally
>agree with that, but hey - a painting today isn't much different from
>a painting in the 1600's - just different inks and paints.

Y'ain't never seen how I paint... but, that aside, there's a difference 
between painting, now, and taking a 17th-century artwork and altering it 
to show one's own signature.

>Or
>electronic analogs of the same, al la Photoshop. ;)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>usage and plagiarism.

Common use is just that... intentionally removing attribution and 
presenting something of a known source as being otherwise might well be 
the first step towards common use, granted, but it still is not common 
use; consider the Academic Maxim of 'Steal from one, plagiarism, steal 
from ten, documented conclusions, steal from a hundred, original thesis.'

DD
```

```
PR wrote:
> On Nov 19, 7:41 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article
…[82 more quoted lines elided]…
> people I respect and like.

Don't kid yourself, Paul. Master Dwarf and I have been in opposite corners 
for decades...:-)

However, for some unaccountable reason, I have to admit I'd miss him if he 
wasn't here... (I think it's called "respect" but don't tell him I said 
so...) If you do a search on "mark the calendar" you may find this is not 
the only time said calendar has been marked... :-)
>
> Harry and I were great friends, but his nature was to appear as a
> curmudgeon at every opportunity. He was a very fine human being. We
> actually liked each other a lot.

Sometimes, curmudgeonness can be an amusing and likeable trait...
>
> On the other hand, I must point out, there is a difference between
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> recognize it as coming from _The Tempest_.

If we acknowledged Shakespeare every time we used one of his phrases, we 
would spend a lot of time doing it.

Nevertheless, 'tis a consummation devoutly to be wished...:-)

Perhaps, after  nearly 500 years, the evil that men do can be interred with 
their bones and we don't need to worry about anything being rotten in the 
state of Denmark. It is hard to live one's literary life and neither a 
borrower nor a lender be,  but, if above all things we are to be true to 
ourselves, then it is important not to constrain the quality of mercy, which 
droppeth like the gentle rain from heaven, and in my mind's eye, I can let 
brevity be the soul of wit, with a simple acknowledgement of source.

Sometimes, of course the shoe is on the other foot and people steal my own 
originations. In my salad days when I was green, I would cry "Havoc" and let 
slip the dogs of war on those accursed robbers, but the robbed that smiles 
steals something from the thief, and even though I have seen better days, 
yet will I try it to the last. T'is neither here nor there and I've not lost 
a wink of sleep until the game is up.

This is the short and the long of it, if I cannot tell what the dickens his 
name is, then I can't attribute the source. But, if, as luck would have it, 
I remember what's in a name, why, then the world's my oyster and I will give 
the Devil his due.

Perhaps I bear a charmed life, but I'll not budge an inch, until what's done 
is done.

Most of the above are heard in modern English, and all of them originated 
from William Shakespeare. That's just the tip of the iceberg.


>Nor do I feel ethically or
> morally obligated to provide that information each time I use it.
>

Well that's OK, then... :-)

> What Harry always meant was much more along the lines of "say what you
> mean, don't just repeat what the crowd is chanting." This also tied
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> electronic analogs of the same, al la Photoshop. ;)

I really hope you're wrong about this, Paul... :-)
>
> Also, there is a huge difference between using a saying like that, and
> claiming to have originated it. That is the difference between common
> usage and plagiarism.

That is certainly one difference... :-)

Pete.
```

## Thread: OS/VS Cobol COPY Statement Syntax — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### OS/VS Cobol COPY Statement Syntax

- **From:** John Della-Torre <johndel@nospam.internode.on.net>
- **Date:** 2009-11-19T11:45:53+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<03149156$0$1305$c3e8da3@news.astraweb.com>`

```
I am running some os/vs cobol source through CCCA convert and
it's producing the error:

ABJ6404 Copy Statement has invalid syntax. COPY statement not converted

on the source:

COPY IDMS SUBSCHEMA-BINDS.

and various other COPY statements.

Does anyone know what the two parms mean and how to change the COPY
statement into something acceptable to Enterprise Cobol for z/OS?

All the COPY statements have IDMS as the 1st parm with different values
for the 2nd parm.  I'm guessing that IDMS is the member name in the
//SYSLIB concatenation, but the 2nd parm is a mystery.

The output from the job has been sent to me by a colleague as I don't
have an ID to the system.

Thanks
```

```
> I am running some os/vs cobol source through CCCA convert and
> it's producing the error:

IDMS, now there is a blast from the past.

It may be that the source should be pre-processed and the COPY IDMS
```

#### ↳ Re: OS/VS Cobol COPY Statement Syntax

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-11-21T12:08:22-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v0WNm.249033$Jp1.127754@en-nntp-06.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<03149156$0$1305$c3e8da3@news.astraweb.com>`

```
If you expect to use IDMS on the converted code, then I would "comment" all 
the COPY IDMS statments before running the source thru CCCA.  Then uncomment 
it before compileing with Enterpir COBOL (probably with an IDMS 
preprocessor).  If you do NOT expect to use IDMS with the output source 
code, then you will probably need to figure out what "conersion" tools are 
available for changing the IDMS source code - and do that before using CCCA.

NOTE:
   CCCA is "sensitive" to the LANGLVL compiler option which impacts the COPY 
statement.  Make cerain you know what LANGLVL value is used with the OS/VS 
COBOL compiler before using CCCA.  This won't help eiyh your COPY IDMS 
statements, but issomething to be aware of.

"John Della-Torre" <johndel@nospam.internode.on.net> wrote in message 
news:03149156$0$1305$c3e8da3@news.astraweb.com...
>I am running some os/vs cobol source through CCCA convert and
> it's producing the error:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks
```

```
John
```

```
Well, yes and no.  Again, it depends on what you are going to do with the 
converted source code.

If you plan on continuing to use IDMS, your "converted" output will be a 
problem (as it won't THEN go thru IDSM processing correclty (most of the 
time).

If you do NOT plan on using IDMS, I think the precompiler will add some 
"call" statements that will be a problem.
```

#### ↳ Re: OS/VS Cobol COPY Statement Syntax

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-11-30T07:50:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gdm7h5p2e5u1j7ngsgqb3a26hmmbjj5506@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<03149156$0$1305$c3e8da3@news.astraweb.com>`

```
On Thu, 19 Nov 2009 11:45:53 +1000, John Della-Torre
<johndel@nospam.internode.on.net> wrote:

>COPY IDMS SUBSCHEMA-BINDS.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>statement into something acceptable to Enterprise Cobol for z/OS?

I know what it means.    But the only way to change it is to run it
through the IDMS pre-compiler.

IDMS COPY statements don't use standard COBOL COPY libraries, but use
the IDMS data dictionary.    

Here's an example of an IDMS command after compiling:

036500*    OBTAIN CALC IAARCAA                                     
036600*        ON DB-REC-NOT-FOUND                                 
                 MOVE 3 TO DML-SEQUENCE                            
                 CALL 'IDMS' USING SUBSCHEMA-CTRL                  
                         IDBMSCOM (32)                             
                         SR1456                                    
                         IDBMSCOM (43)                             
                 IF NOT DB-REC-NOT-FOUND PERFORM IDMS-STATUS;      
                 ELSE                                              
036700             DISPLAY 'NO IAARCAA FOUND FOR "'                
036800                           FINDER-REC(1:9) '"'               
036900         END-IF.                                             

The sequence numbers are in the source code, showing you what it
looked like.    The comments were added by the pre-compiler when it
replaced those two lines with the code that does not contain sequence
numbers.

One thing that this means is that I cannot have IDMS commands in lines
conditionally commented out with a "D" in column 7.    That's because
the pre-compiler doesn't parse this out, and the regular compiler
doesn't understand the IDMS command.

Depending on your precompiler options the following command may save
displaying the precompiler output:

000100 *NODMLIST                 
000200  IDENTIFICATION DIVISION. 
000300   

Alternatively to show it:
000100 *DMLIST                 
000200  IDENTIFICATION DIVISION. 
000300                           

Otherwise, only the precompiler errors show up. 
```

<a id="t-a0671b354d"></a>
## Thread: IBM COBOL Migration to Windows COBOL — 15 this month / 30 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### IBM COBOL Migration to Windows COBOL

- **From:** Chuckles55 <chuckmoore55@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-11-24T10:30:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89888226-4792-45d5-b19b-c304f94250c1@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com>`

```
Hi Everyone,

My organization wants to move its IBM z/OS COBOL programs off onto
another (perceived-to-be cheaper) platform. One option being discussed
is converting the (400+) programs (most of which do financial
crunching to create print files) to run on Windows using either
MicroFocus COBOL or Fujitsu NETCOBOL. There will be 2-4 programmers on
the conversion team.
My questions:
1) Does anyone here have a preference between MicroFocus and Fujitsu ?
2) If anyone has done conversions like this, how were DD statement
(let alone IDCAMS) functionality converted ? (i.e. pass file
descriptions in as XML ?, use ISAM on Windows ?)

Any further comments or suggestions would be appreciated,
Chuck
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM COBOL Migration to Windows COBOL

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-11-26T00:48:01+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7n4jriF3jqndeU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<89888226-4792-45d5-b19b-c304f94250c1@v15g2000prn.googlegroups.com>`

```
Chuckles55 wrote:
> Hi Everyone,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1) Does anyone here have a preference between MicroFocus and Fujitsu ?

Far too much to cover in detail here.

1. Fujitsu has no runtime fees and you do not require the .NET COBOL 
compiler. The software is excellent. (So is MicroFocus, but my preference 
would be Fujitsu.)

> 2) If anyone has done conversions like this, how were DD statement
> (let alone IDCAMS) functionality converted ? (i.e. pass file
> descriptions in as XML ?, use ISAM on Windows ?)

1. Automatically convert the VSAM (KSDS) Files to Relational Database (Data 
Conversion - fully automated).

2. Load your existing VSAM data to the new table sets. (Load Module 
generation and Loading - fully automated)

That completes data conversion. Now you can access your DB with standard 
software as well as COBOL. The report programs can be systematically 
replaced with tools designed for reporting (Crystal Reports, StimulSoft, 
etc.)... or not. Your choice. At least you have some options...

3. Generate a Data Access Layer of Objects to access the RDB table sets. 
(DAL objects in OO COBOL that carry out all the access the VSAM verbs used 
to.) These objects are generated from your RDB table set definitions. This 
separates the data layer from the Business logic without any real effort on 
your part. The objects are multi-threaded and can be invoked easily from 
COBOL and any other languages that support the Component Object Model. You 
can access your new DB easily from new COBOL apps, Web Pages, or new apps 
written in new languages, separately or conjointly. (fully automated)

4. Amend the application programs to replace all the VSAM access with 
invokes of the DAL objects generated in 3 above. (over 95% automated - some 
minor manual amendments in extreme cases, for very complex or non-COBOL 85 
compliant code.)

At this point both Data and Code Conversion is complete. You have a system 
that runs on the new networked platform under .NET, accesses a relational 
database in a separate tier, and the existing applications function exactly 
as they always have, except that now they are accessing the new RDB instead 
of VSAM KSDS.

(Although you CAN use ISAM under Windows as a VSAM equivalent, Relational 
Databases are preferable because they are open, standardised, and scalable.) 
Of course, if you can get them automatically, without any manual effort, 
generated from your exisiting COBOL copybooks, that is just another reason 
for going that way. The Toolset generates normalised DB tables, along with 
modules to load them, all from your existing legacy COBOL codebase, in 
seconds.

NEW applications can be developed on the Web or .NET using COBOL or whatever 
other languages you may want to use.

If you are currently using CICS, you may want to replace this with ASP.NET 
or WPF or simple WinForms. Fujitsu offer NeoKICKS, but this DOES require the 
.NET COBOL compiler.

The converted system runs under .NET using SQL Server or your RDBMS of 
choice.

>
> Any further comments or suggestions would be appreciated,

Please see http://primacomputing.co.nz/cobol21/mig.aspx and 
http://primacomputing.co.nz/cobol21/PRIMAToolset.aspx which describe the 
background to the outline above.

My personal opinion:

It is fairly easy to "transfer" an existing COBOL system to a different 
platform.  (Whether that platform is networked workstations or a new central 
processing site.) However doing this simply takes time, money, and energy 
and provides no LONG TERM relief. You might as well address conversion as a 
complete Migration, and go for objects and layers which will see you well 
into the future (as well as being much more viable in today's networked 
environments.) Objects and layers allow you to use other languages and tools 
besides COBOL. Re-factoring your existing code means you can usually (not 
always...) save a good percentage of the cost of rebuilding everything from 
scratch.

I started moving off COBOL around 4 years ago and encountered all the 
problems described on the web site. I started writing tools to help me, and 
since then have assisted other people to undertake the same migration. Today 
I develop in C#, but I still have a number of legacy COBOL components that 
are still doing the jobs they have always done. As time and money permits, 
I'll gradually replace them, but refactoring this functionality into objects 
has bought me time and eased the transition into "New Technology".

The PRIMA Toolset automatically creates objects and layers from existing 
legacy code. I don't know any other conversion/migration tools that do that.

Good luck with whatever you decide.

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM COBOL Migration to Windows COBOL

```
On Nov 25, 5:48 am, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

Pete, what are your methods for converting batch applications and JCL
```
